data.title: A Golden Era For Income Investing - 2 High Yield Picks I Like
data.description:
Introduction

As I write this, there's a ton of uncertainty surrounding the U.S. economy. As someone who was bullish on the overall market, mainly due to us seeing multiple interest rate cuts this year, now I'm not so sure.

However, in chaos comes opportunity. And as long as I can remember, now may be one of the best times to be an income-oriented investor. I've stated previously that investors should have at least 2 portfolios minimum.

And one of those should be solely focused on income. With the likelihood for the market to see muted returns for at least the near to medium-term, this article highlights why now may be a good time to be an income-focused investor, and two high yield picks I like and why.

Uncertainty Rising

In 2025, although we saw uncertainty, the S&P (SP500) still managed to close up over 16%, solid all things considered. The market was led by the Technology (XLK) sector due to AI enthusiasm.

Through the first three plus months of 2026, things have seemed to shift. Now, the market seems to believe the economy may be in worse shape than advertised. Me, personally, I never believed the economy was in all that good of shape.

Mainly due to what I've personally witnessed from family & friends. And going forward, I think things may get worse before they get better. We also are currently at war with Iran. And at the time of writing, Iran has rejected peace talks.

Currently, Iran has laid out 5 conditions for the war to end. The question is will the U.S. & Israel accept. My belief is no. And the longer the war goes on, the worse things will likely get.

At the time of writing, Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, causing oil & gas prices to rise. There's also the potential to see the U.S. send in ground troops into Iran. If this happens, I foresee market volatility increasing and potentially entering bear market territory.

This is because inflation fears will increase, dimming rate cut hopes. Not to mention, mortgage rates are back on the rise, resulting in affordability issues for a lot of Americans.

As a result, it's likely the broader market will underperform, or at least muted returns for the near to medium-term. But the two picks I mention stand to benefit from the market's potential underperformance. Without further ado, let's get into the two high yields.

Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) - 8.81% Yield

The first high yield fund I think is attractive is GPIX by Goldman Sachs (GS). Unlike some of their peers, I like that fact the GPIX actually holds equities instead of using a synthetic strategy to get exposure.

In my opinion, this makes them attractive as a potential long-term holding. GPIX's top 10 holdings include behemoths like NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META).

As a result, Technology (XLK) is their largest sector at 33%. And while tech stocks have underperformed recently, rapid AI adoption will likely benefit GPIX long-term.

The most attractive metric about GPIX and why I think they can potentially outperform is their covered call strategy. And in sideways markets, like now, the fund is able to harness higher options premiums. This is because GPIX only sells options of a portion of their portfolio.

Typically, this is between 25% to 75% of the portfolio, leaving the rest uncovered. This is different than peer, the NEOS S&P High Income ETF (SPYI), who typically sells options on 100% of its assets.

While this allows GPIX to participate in more upside in bull markets, they are still limited due to their covered call strategy. This is why they're likely to underperform.

This also allows them to continue paying a solid distribution as they harness higher options premiums. As a result, they can potentially outperform the index in total returns.

While many covered funds usually see lower distributions in volatile markets due to NAV erosion, this is where GPIX's superior strategy outshines its peers. Year-to-date, the S&P (SP500) is down 3.7% at the time of writing. GPIX is down 4.37%, while SPYI is down 5.17%.

But GPIX's monthly distributions make them a worthy investment in the current environment. In the months of January and February, the ETF paid monthly distributions of $0.3755 and $0.3763. For comparison, during the first 3 months, SPYI distributions declined modestly.

In January, SPYI paid out $0.5309. Next they paid $0.5219 and for March $0.5104. I also like the fact that GPIX has a lower expense ratio at 0.35%. This is compared to 0.68% for SPYI. But SPYI's distribution yield is much higher at 12.04%, compared to 8.81% for GPIX.

Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) - 10.95% Yield

Sticking with Goldman Sachs, GPIQ is the second fund I would consider. The only difference between GPIQ and GPIX is the indexes they track. GPIQ follows the Nasdaq (NDAQ), making them more tech heavy.

As a result, the sector makes up roughly 51% of the portfolio. Another key difference is the Communications Services (XLC) sector make up the second largest (sector) instead of the Financials (XLF).

Going forward, GPIQ is likely to outperform long-term as many remain bullish on the rapid growth of AI adoption in the U.S. Because they follow the NDAQ, GPIQ only has 105 holdings compared to 499 for GPIX.

GPIQ has the same expense ratio at 0.35% and uses a dynamic options strategy, selling options on a portion of the portfolio. This allows for greater upside participation in bull markets.

But as previously mentioned, the funds are likely to underperform in bull markets. But in down or flat markets, this is where both funds are designed to shine as they are able to generate higher options premiums.

Year-to-date, GPIQ has fared better than NEOS' Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), who's down over 6%. JPMorgan's Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) has seen less of a drawdown compared to both.

As far as distributions, JEPQ has seen theirs decline modestly. In January, the ETF paid a monthly distribution of $0.5761. In February, this was $0.4657 and increased in March to $0.5090.

QQQI's declined on sequential basis from $0.6359 in January, $0.6140 in February, and $0.6089 in March. While this may not seem significant, it can be for someone strictly focused on income.

For comparison, GPIQ's distributions declined month over month from $0.4655 in February to $0.4527 in March. For January, their distribution in $0.4633.

Why These Two Picks

Because of uncertainty increasing, investors may be wondering why these two funds since the S&P and Nasdaq are both down YTD. Particularly, the NDAQ is down double-digits, close to 14%.

Because of their options strategies leaving a portion of their portfolios uncovered in bull markets, I believe this makes GPIX & GPIQ superior funds for long-term holdings. They also have lower expense ratios than most peers, which can eat into an investor's returns long-term.

You can see from the chart below that both GPIX & GPIQ outperformed some of their peers in total returns in 2025. Only SPYI was able to outperform GPIX, while GPIQ outperformed both QQQI & JEPQ.

While past performance is no indicator of future performance, I think the market uncertainty is likely to last a while longer. This is mainly because of the ongoing war. And having an income-focused portfolio can blunt the blow from the potential to see underperformance in the overall market.

Plus you can collect higher yields that deliver reliable and stable income. Which is essential during periods of uncertainty like now. Both GPIX and GPIQ have also seen stable NAV growth due to their lower distribution yields.

While some investors may prefer funds with significantly higher yields, GPIX & GPIQ's superior covered call strategies make them ideal for holding long-term, not just during periods of uncertainty.

And lastly, because they both use 1256 contracts, both funds' distributions are tax favorable. The chart below shows how their distributions were taxed for fiscal year 2025. GPIQ had a higher amount of their distributions considered return of capital at close to 96%. GPIX's distributions were approximately 88% ROC.

Risks & Conclusion

Although I consider GPIX and GPIQ superior funds, both are not immune to underperformance.

As you can see from above, both are also down YTD due to increased volatility. And while both were designed to outperform in down or flat markets, a longer war has the potential to push us into a recession.

If so, both funds' yields will likely not save them from seeing negative total returns. This is something investors just be aware of going forward.

But if you're someone looking to position your portfolio to deliver reliable income during market volatility and hold long-term, then I believe GPIX & GPIQ are the best positioned funds for the job.

data.title: PVH: Setup Is Not Good Enough To Justify An Upgrade To Buy
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on PVH Corp. (PVH) was a hold rating because I was concerned about the near-term earnings pressure given the tariff situation, lack of revenue growth, and geopolitical risk in China. In this update, I still think a neutral stance makes sense. The positive is that PVH’s turnaround work looks more credible now, with better brand execution and stronger product campaigns. The issue is that underlying growth is still not strong enough, and tariff headwinds remain visible.

4Q25 results update

PVH reported Q4 2025 revenue of $2.5 billion, which was a 6% y/y growth on a reported basis but flat in constant currency [CC]. By region, EMEA revenue grew 8% on a reported basis but fell 3% CC; Americas revenue grew 4%; and APAC revenue was flat on a reported basis and down 2% CC. One thing to note is that the APAC result included a ~4% decline from the Lunar New Year timing shift as the holiday fell in Q4 2024 but not in Q4 2025. In terms of brand performance, Tommy Hilfiger revenue grew 7% reported and 1% CC; Calvin Klein revenue grew 3% reported but fell 1% CC. By channel, direct-to-consumer [DTC] revenue grew 1% reported and fell 3% CC, while owned and operated digital commerce revenue grew 5% reported and was flat CC. Wholesale revenue did better with 11% reported growth and 4% CC. However, growth did not translate into bottom line growth directly. Adj. EPS was only up slightly from $3.27 to $3.82. The main reason was the gross margin decline due to higher tariffs on goods entering the US, the transition of previously licensed women’s categories, and a more promotional environment.

The turnaround progress

Previously, my positive view was that PVH's brand-building actions were starting to work and that the Calvin Klein operating issues were getting resolved. Those notions continue to advance in the right direction as management is now talking about on-time deliveries, margins back on plan at Calvin Klein, stronger spring sell-through across both brands and all three regions, and a third consecutive season of positive order books in Europe. Relative to things a few quarters ago, this is a much cleaner story, in my view.

Take Calvin Klein, for instance. The core products (underwear and denim) continue to see good demand, but importantly, the newer product and campaign work seems to be landing better. One of these newer initiatives is Icon Cotton Stretch, which grew 20% in men’s and 13% in women’s. Another example is fashion denim, which management noted is now >50% of Calvin’s denim business and grew high single digits. Besides the positive growth numbers, which are great, I think the key takeaway here is that PVH is able to pair its core products with major talent (i.e., famous people) and strong marketing to capture more demand. The best supporting evidence is the Jungkook campaign, which drove sales of campaign items up >50% after launch, and the jackets he wore reached 60% sell-through in two weeks.

We see a similar pattern in Tommy Hilfiger. The brand saw strong growth in the cable-knit sweater franchise, with sales up >50% in Q4. Similarly to Calvin Klein, where PVH leveraged a single talent, the Tommy Hilfiger brand leveraged the Liverpool brand (a partnership), which drove a 200% increase in sales for tunnel-walk products in Europe vs. the previous week. The same was seen in China, where Tommy DTC sales were up double digits in March after local activations around the Formula 1 partnership.

The common pattern between the two is that PVH will focus on core categories, create visible brand moments (leveraging an existing entity that has a strong brand), and turn those moments into traffic and sell-through. Importantly, PVH has been successfully repeating this strategy, and that gives comfort that PVH is in the growth driver seat now.

The setup is better, but it is still not clean enough for a bullish call

That said, the setup is not entirely clean. The first problem is growth quality. As much as I see a repeatable strategy that is promising, Q4 revenue was flat CC. Calvin Klein was even down 1% CC, and DTC was also down 3% CC. Across regions, EMEA was also down 3% and APAC was down 2% CC. Americas did perform better, but remember that it benefited from taking previously licensed women’s categories in-house. I need to see actual growth accelerate (CC basis) in order to be convinced that PVH’s strategy is indeed working to drive growth.

The second problem is that the next leg of the story still depends on external factors' pressure easing. Even if PVH continues to execute really well, the tariffs' impact needs to go away in order for the P&L to reflect that success. But that is not the case, apparently. Management is guiding to ~215 bps gross tariff impact in 2026, with a gross EBIT hit of ~$195 million, which translates to ~$3.30 per share, before mitigation. They did say to mitigate ~60% of that this year and exit the year at >75% mitigation on an annualized basis, but the key word is annualized. Which means this could be something they achieved in Q4, and the actual impact would be much more than that 75% implies. So, PVH can do a lot right operationally and still have the reported numbers look weak because the tariff drag is just so big.

Moreover, I am particularly worried about the gap between brand momentum and channel confidence. Management said wholesalers remain cautious and the consumer environment is still not exactly robust. While Europe's order books are positive, which is clearly better than before, in-season replenishment was weak in Q4, which implies wholesalers are not expecting a strong demand ahead. APAC wholesale partners are also still taking a cautious approach as well. This tells me the demand strength is not exactly in the “strong” territory.

Valuation

I still don’t think we are going to see any major upward re-rating to the multiples here. There are tangible changes in core brands, but Q4 growth was really not that strong, and the FY2026 revenue guide basically tells us growth is not going to accelerate hard. Moreover, wholesalers remain cautious, which indicates that underlying demand may not be as strong. PVH's current forward PE is ~6x for a business that is expected to grow its topline by ~low-single-digits, which seems fair when I compare it to Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF), which trades at ~8.6x with mid-single-digit revenue growth expectations.

Conclusion

I am maintaining a hold rating on PVH. I do think the business is moving in the right direction. But I am not ready to get bullish yet. CC growth was still weak in Q4, wholesalers remain cautious, and the tariff impact in 2026 is large enough to weigh on earnings even if execution improves further. For now, I think PVH looks better as a business, but the stock is not that attractive yet.

data.title: Navigating The Hormuz Crisis
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Holiday reminder: Wall Street Breakfast won't be published tomorrow, with markets closed for Good Friday.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Tariff watch: The White House is preparing to overhaul steel and aluminum tariffs, and eyes new levies on certain drugmakers.

Code leak: Anthropic (ANTHRO) is working to contain the leak of some internal source code for its AI agent Claude Code.

Renewed optimism: Recession odds on prediction market Kalshi (KALSHI) plummet after better-than-expected economic data.

Way forward

President Donald Trump has quashed market hopes for a quick end to the Middle East war, saying the U.S. will strike Iran "extremely hard" over the next two to three weeks. That likely means the Strait of Hormuz will continue to remain effectively closed, sending stock markets lower and oil prices higher once again.

Long road ahead: Trump said the U.S. will consider a ceasefire when the Strait of Hormuz is open and clear. "Until then, we are blasting Iran into oblivion or, as they say, back to the Stone Ages!!!" he declared. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the future of the waterway would be decided jointly by Iran and Oman after the war ends, and not by external powers. "From our point of view, the strait is currently open and is closed only to those who are at war with Iran," Araghchi clarified. Iran has been imposing significant fees, reportedly up to $2M per tanker in some cases, for safe passage, with payments often coordinated in non-U.S. dollar currencies such as Chinese yuan.

Global response: The UK will host a summit of 35 countries to "assess all viable diplomatic and political measures we can take to restore freedom of navigation [in the Strait of Hormuz], guarantee the safety of trapped ships and seafarers, and to resume the movement of vital commodities," UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer said. Meanwhile, Gulf Arab states are considering new export pipelines to bypass the Strait of Hormuz despite high costs and complexities that stalled similar plans in the past. Viable near-term options include the expansion of Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline and Abu Dhabi's existing route to Fujairah. Another option could be reviving plans for the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor, with Israel's Haifa Port serving as a major gateway.

SA commentary: Investing Group Leader Bret Jensen warned that even if the Strait of Hormuz was reopened fully, the damaged assets in the region could take months – even years – to bring back online. Moreover, the oil reserves that have been released by countries will need to be replenished over time. "I expect disruption to global energy supply to last even after a ceasefire or a peace deal," said SA analyst Geneva Investor. "This disruption cannot be understated, as it can be quantified as almost twice as large as that of the 1973 oil crisis overall." Hormuz Situation Creates 'Strait-Line' Case For BNO Over USO

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Back To Bullion: Top Gold Stocks For The Yellow Metal Rebound

Sell The Rally - There Is No Easy Way Out Of Iran War

Amazon: This Is Worse Than You Think

Alphabet's Solution Is A Gift For Micron's Memory Sales

2 Undervalued 8-9% Yields The Market Is Sleeping On After Energy's Huge Rally

What else is happening...

Trump floats NATO exit again as rift with European allies deepens.

Lucas drones, inspired by Iran's tech, signal U.S. strategy shift.

Lilly (LLY) CEO opposes codification of Trump drug pricing deals.

Globalstar rallies on report of Amazon (AMZN) eyeing acquisition.

SpaceX (SPACE) files for IPO, plans to host analyst day in April.

Intel (INTC) to buy back 49% stake in Ireland fab JV for $14.2B.

Perplexity accused of sharing user search data with Google, META.

Apple (AAPL) maintains shine at 50 as it cautiously enters AI era.

Artemis II lifts off, sending astronauts on journey toward the moon.

Is Beijing constructing a new military base in the South China Sea?

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -2.4%. Hong Kong -0.7%. China -0.7%. India +0.3%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.3%. Paris -1.2%. Frankfurt -2.1%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -1.1%. S&P -1.3%. Nasdaq -1.7%. Crude +7.8% to $107.91. Gold -3.5% to $4,642.90. Bitcoin -3% to $66,427.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +4 bps to 4.37%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include American Resources (AREC) and Acuity (AYI).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Rakuten: Value Hidden Behind A Telecom Overhang
data.description:
Overview

Rakuten Group (RKUNY) is a Japan-based global internet conglomerate whose primary positioning is the creation of the internet ecosystem consisting of over 70 services which includes online retail, travel, credit cards, banking, securities, and mobile. This business model has a significant impact in terms of having a unified user id system and more cross-selling opportunities which leads to a higher customer lifetime value. The list of companies are given below for each of the spaces.

The Fintech segment serves as the primary profit engine for Rakuten Group while the Internet Services drives user growth and the mobile business acts as the long-term customer acquisition platform and data generation. By leveraging data, AI, and cross-platform synergies, Rakuten aims to create a self-reinforcing digital ecosystem that enhances user retention, monetization, and long-term growth. What acts as a key tailwind for Rakuten’s business is the ecosystem model ensures that consumer stickiness remains high as shifting away from Rakuten implies looking at not one but multiple such substitutes for each of the services procured under Rakuten.

The figure below shows the three major business segments that Rakuten officially reports and the proportion of sales that they take up along with their revenue growth. The mobile segment takes up the smallest proportion of sales, but it is also delivering strong growth in terms of sales while the highest sales growth is coming from FinTech.

Rakuten’s segments are not just diversified—they are strategically interdependent. The mobile segment is utilized for the purpose of ecosystem expansion and future growth, internet services is used for user acquisition and engagement and the fintech segment is used as the profit machine. Therefore, Rakuten’s FinTech business is essentially subsidizing its internet and mobile businesses.

However, each of these segments also have more granular business segments as well that is worth noting and they operate in their independent ways as well which makes Rakuten a multi-layered ecosystem where each business segment operates in a symbiotic relation and that ensures that Rakuten has a solidly defensible business model which, along with other internal and external factors, makes for a potential investment opportunity for the average long-term investor.

Rakuten Group represents a fintech-driven ecosystem trading at a structural discount due to its capital-intensive mobile segment. While the FinTech business generates strong and growing profits, the Mobile segment has historically depressed group profitability. However, improving mobile unit economics, rising ARPU, and increasing ecosystem monetization create potential for meaningful valuation re-rating if execution continues.

This investment thesis hinges on two key assumptions - 1) sustained growth and profitability of the FinTech segment, and 2) gradual reduction in losses from the Mobile business.

All financial figures are reported in Japanese Yen (JPY) based on company disclosures. USD conversions, where used, assume an exchange rate of approximately ¥150/USD. Growth rates may vary depending on whether total segment revenue or service-specific revenue is considered. For example, mobile segment growth may differ across disclosures depending on classification of total revenue versus service revenue.

Tailwind

Rakuten Positioned to Grow with Fintech in Japan

One of the strongest positives working in favour of Rakuten is their cash machine being the fintech business which is experiencing and is expected to go through tremendous growth in the coming years. Currently 40% of the transactions in Japan are cashless and the target is to reach 80%. With such a target set in mind, Rakuten needs to position itself as one of the leaders in the fintech space which it already has. Rakuten figures in the list along with other names like PayPay Card, Estie, Datachain, etc.

Rakuten’s major source of revenue comes from Rakuten Cards and Rakuten Bank both of which are expected to benefit from the upcoming external changes. Rakuten Cards is expected to benefit from the growth in the transaction volume, higher average spend per user.

The cards business is expected to grow at 6.1% CAGR from 2025 to 2033. Apart from the increasing trend towards cashless transactions, there is also the positive push towards electronic transactions from the regulatory authorities as well to enable financial inclusion by increasing collaboration between bank and non-bank players in the financial space as well. This is likely to benefit Rakuten further due to its complete ecosystem-based business model which will lead to deriving significant synergies.

This space also holds several opportunities like integration of AI and blockchain in payment fraud detection. Today, Rakuten is positioned strongly in implementation of AI because it has already implemented the same in marketing efficiency, operations and customer engagement.

Another opportunity available for the Japanese payments market is implementing BNPL (buy now pay later), especially for the younger population (20-35). Rakuten has indirect exposure to this opportunity because it already owns the foundations that are required – merchant relationships, user base and payment rails. Rakuten Card system already works as a form of revolving credit and instalment payments for the e-commerce platform.

Hence Rakuten’s positioning in the fintech space along with the ecosystem positioning puts it right in the middle of the emerging opportunities in the fintech space in Japan.

While the financial inclusion measures are expected to benefit Rakuten Cards, the broader macroeconomic and financial scenario is expected to benefit Rakuten Bank as well. The net interest margin is expected to increase as Bank of Japan (BoJ) provides indication of raising the rates. For example, a report from S&P Global states that Japan’s megabanks. The figure below highlights the same over the recent quarters as well.

Tsuyoshi Ueno, a senior economist at NLI Research Institute, stated that BoJ will continue its rate hike cycle unless there is a clear risk that emerges. This implies that banks will be able to earn higher net interest margin.

Moreover, note that the Bank of Japan (BoJ) has signalled a gradual normalization of the monetary policy with rate hikes becoming increasingly plausible. According to central bank commentary and market expectations, this shift could benefit Rakuten Bank through expansion in net interest margins.

Hence, both the factors are likely to come together to ensure that the fintech business of Rakuten Group continues to evolve and grow, driving the overall profitability of the group.

Scaling of the Mobile Business

As it was previously mentioned, the mobile business acts as a customer acquisition engine for the overall group rather than being a standalone business. Rakuten had entered the space via greenfield investment, building a fully virtualized network from scratch. This led to significant operating losses in the subsequent years (explored further).

The mobile segment recorded EBITDA of 28.8 billion yen ($180.69 million), an improvement of 65.1 billion yen ($408.44 million) YoY in FY25 and subscriber base crossed 10 million. Telecom is a business which has 3 stages – build, scale and profit. Currently Rakuten Mobile is in the scaling phase. The impact of the increase in subscriber base is given below in the two figures.

Rakuten Mobile has credible backing it with major names like Cisco, Nokia and F5 being its technology partners.

Adding to the already existing tailwinds of Rakuten Group, there is the further optimistic trend of the ARPU (average revenue per user). Rakuten Group’s quarterly financial reporting highlights this trend and underlines the fact that this is not a one-off event. Rakuten’s quarterly disclosures indicate that net ARPU reached approximately ¥2,467 (≈$16 at ¥150/USD), reflecting a year-on-year increase. This consistent improvement in ARPU highlights improving monetization of the mobile user base. Similarly, for Q3, FY25 net ARPU witnessed a growth of $0.69 YoY. This goes on to highlight the consistency of the growth.

Financials & Valuation

The figure below displays the financials of Rakuten – the sales value and profit margins. What is most conspicuous is that the profit margins dipped post 2018. One of the primary reasons for this is Rakuten’s investment in the mobile business. The investment in the mobile business led to high capex, heavy marketing spend and high operating costs. Therefore, the change in profit margins is not a cyclical development, but a structural change in business itself. Previously, Rakuten was following a relatively asset-light model and now it follows a relatively more capex-heavy model.

Moreover, revenue growth has also slowed since FY20. This has been largely because of the maturing of the other businesses. While the mobile segment is a new business, it still constitutes of a smaller proportion of the total business of Rakuten Group.

To begin with the valuation exercise, I performed a valuation for the entire group using some of the key ratios. Due to the negative and volatile cash flows, it was not possible to perform a DCF valuation. The snapshot of the exercise is given below.

The relative valuation suggests potential undervaluation, although this remains sensitive to assumptions around mobile profitability and peer multiples, it is a signal for investors to make money on the underpriced stock given the prevailing tailwinds and market conditions. Each of the ratios show there is substantial upside for Rakuten Group.

However, given the different business segments of Rakuten Group each of the segments have very different economic characteristics. The FinTech segment is earnings-driven and comparable to financial institutions, the Internet Services segment resembles platform businesses, and the Mobile segment follows telecom economics. A single valuation approach would fail to capture the differences accurately.

A more substantial sum of the parts valuation provides very high upside when considering the three business segments – fintech, internet services and mobile. The SOTP valuation gives a healthy upside. The net income for each of the segments is not disclosed separately so the net income of fintech segment has been estimated separately.

The valuation multiples are derived for each segment's peer groups. FinTech is benchmarked against Japanese financial institutions and global fintech firms. Internet Services against global e-commerce platforms. Mobile against telecom operators.

A sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation requires benchmarking each segment against appropriate peer groups reflecting similar business economics.This is why the FinTech segment is benchmarked against Japanese financial institutions such as SBI Holdings, Mizuho Financial Group, and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group, as well as global fintech players like Visa and PayPal, reflecting its earnings-driven and balance sheet-oriented nature.

The Internet Services segment is compared with global platform and e-commerce companies such as Amazon, eBay, and Booking Holdings, which share similar marketplace and advertising-driven revenue models.

The Mobile segment is benchmarked against telecom operators such as NTT Docomo, KDDI, and SoftBank, given its capital-intensive infrastructure and subscriber-based revenue model.

This segmentation ensures that each business is valued using relevant market benchmarks rather than applying a uniform multiple across the group.

Different valuation multiples are applied to each segment based on their underlying business characteristics. The FinTech segment is valued using P/E multiple, reflecting its stable earnings profile. The Internet Services segment is valued using EV/EBITDA multiple, consistent with platform businesses. The Mobile segment is valued using EV/EBITDA, given its evolving profitability profile.

The difference between peer-based valuation and SOTP valuation arises primarily due to the conglomerate structure of Rakuten. While group valuation assigns a uniform multiple, SOTP valuation assigns a higher value to the profit-making fintech segment which raises the valuation of Rakuten Group overall. The total enterprise value was calculated after summing individual enterprise values of each segment. The equity value was derived from enterprise value after subtracting net debt and then dividing by the number of shares outstanding.

Therefore, both the valuation point towards the same direction. There is a handsome opportunity available for investors to invest in Rakuten Group. However, there are certain risks that need to be considered as well.

Headwinds

Persistent Negative Profits Driven by Mobile Segment

One of the biggest pullbacks for Rakuten Group is the consistent negative profits that the group is earning. This adversely impacts valuation as well which goes onto negatively impact investor perception.

The net income has consistently remained negative and the losses from the mobile business continues to more than offset the positive profits from fintech and internet businesses. This implies that the mobile segment continues to be a drag on the overall group’s profitability and is therefore, a cause of concern.

While there have been arguments that the mobile segment is meant to be a machine for user acquisition and is expected to turn positive in profits, there have been mentions about further investments likely to be made about the segment. Moreover, it is worth noting that telecom is not an easy business to set a foot in. Rakuten is competing against players like NTT Docomo SoftBank Corp and so on which have already established infrastructure, deep pockets and sticky pricing and plans. In previous discussions, the claims about inflection point was raised based on EBITDA becoming positive. However, for the telecom business, depreciation is a major cost. So, net profit remains a cause of concern.

Moreover, while subscriber growth is high, it does not automatically imply profitability because one needs to look at churn rate, cost per subscriber and so on. However, further analysis revealed that the churn rate for Rakuten Mobile improved from 5.4% to 1.33%, warranting the optimism regarding the segment.

So, currently the mobile segment displays mixed signals, it is clearly a high stakes game that Rakuten is playing and could be massive value unlock for the group or a major pull-down for the business.

Low Mix of Equity in Funding

Rakuten currently has very low mix of equity in its funding as indicated by the assets-liability graph below. While this leads to a very low cost of capital, the cost of distress goes up. At a time when profits are still negative, this can be a concerning trend when interest payments add further pressure on the bottom line, adversely impact valuation.

I dived deeper to understand the cause of the high total liability and what were its components. Across the years the largest component was other current liabilities which consisted of on average 72% of total liabilities. It is accounted for by the payment & settlement liabilities and credit card payables. Whenever a customer makes a payment, the amount is then settled with a time gap with merchants who are on the Rakuten platform. The timing difference creates the short-term liability. Moreover, credit card payables, customer deposits, loyalty points (until redeemed) all sum up to create the high amount of the other current liability. Note that most of the drivers are associated with the Rakuten Fintech business and therefore, the growth of the fintech business has been one of the key drivers of the tremendous growth of the other current liabilities.

However, as of FY25, one of the unique developments has been the massive shift in the source of total liability. As of FY24, $1.18 million out of $1.6 million total liability was part of other current liability whereas in FY25, $1.34 million out of $1.75 million total liability was part of other non-current liability. This represents a tectonic shift in the liability structure. The cause of this is most likely due to reclassification of certain liabilities. The possible reclassification and maturity extension could have a positive impact on the liquidity pressures of Rakuten Group as indicated by improvements in the liquidity ratios like current ratio.

Conclusion

Strategically, Rakuten is entering a critical phase as it balances growth and profitability. The planned ¥200 billion (≈$1.3 billion at ¥150/USD) capex for FY26 reflects continued investment in mobile infrastructure, signaling management’s long-term commitment to achieving scale. Additionally, the CEO’s push toward a unified app aims to deepen ecosystem integration, enhance user engagement, and unlock cross-selling opportunities.

So, Rakuten Group represents a complex but compelling investment case, driven by the contrasting business segments of the profitable fintech business segment and investment-heavy telecom business. The ecosystem model of business provides the benefits of user stickiness and cross-selling opportunities which has multiplying impact on the business of Rakuten Group.

However, the market’s perception of Rakuten is heavily influenced by the sustained losses that the group has made on the mobile business which has required significant investment because it has been a greenfield investment while the Rakuten Fintech business has not yet managed to offset the losses from the mobile business.

But the mobile business holds promise in terms of the growth in subscriber count, more cross-selling opportunities, inflection opportunities in terms of profits. The mobile business even today is a high-risk strategy that Rakuten Group is playing but I believe that the bet will pay off well for the group due to the business signals that are being received.

Therefore, I believe that the group holds promise and potential for investors and therefore this is the right investment opportunity given the downside that the market has currently assigned to Rakuten Group.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Oil Gains After War Talk
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Oil prices jump as Trump threatens to hit Iran ‘extremely hard.’ (00:16) Sleep Number (SNBR) seeks rescue financing to avoid bankruptcy -- report. (01:25) Sam's Club (WMT) raises its annual membership fees - matches BJ's (BJ), still lower than Costco (COST). (02:09)

This is an abridged transcript.

Oil prices are up again after President Trump said the country will strike Iran “extremely hard” over the next two to three weeks.

Oil prices had been down for the last two days as hopes of a potential de-escalation rose after Trump hinted at ending the conflict soon.

As of the time of this recording, crude futures (CL1:COM) are up 7% to $107 a barrel, and Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) are up 7.3% to $108 per barrel.

In a rare prime-time address on Wednesday, Trump portrayed the Iran war as nearing success, saying U.S. forces had largely achieved their objectives, including crippling Iran’s missile capabilities and degrading its air force, navy, and industrial base. He said the campaign would weaken Tehran’s proxies and block its path to nuclear weapons.

Trump signaled a potential escalation in military action, saying the U.S. could intensify strikes over the next two to three weeks and warning Iran would be pushed “back to the stone ages.”

At the same time, he left the door open to diplomacy, saying talks are ongoing.

Sleep Number (SNBR) is down 21% in premarket action.

The company is seeking rescue financing to prevent bankruptcy.

In its annual report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission last month, the company warned that it had “substantial doubt” it could continue as a going concern due to “high debt, weak sales, and expected financial covenant breaches.”

In its most recent quarterly results, the mattress manufacturer reported an 8% decline in fourth-quarter sales and a $32M drop in gross profit due to a $9.6M inventory obsolescence charge.

Over just the past month, shares have lost more than 71% of their value.

Sam's Club announced on Wednesday that it will raise its annual membership fee by $10 to $60 for basic membership and $120 for its higher-tier option. The price hike will be the first from the retailer since it raised annual fees in October 2022. The new price will go into effect on May 1.

In a statement, Sam’s Club noted that it adjusted membership pricing to support the things members love, pointing to the wide assortment, expanded hours, and better curbside pickup and delivery options.

The new fee structure is still less than Costco (COST) but matches BJ's Wholesale Club (BJ).

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Intel to boost stake in SambaNova, chip startup chaired by Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan - report

No deal yet? Trump readies new tariffs on select drugmakers - report

Stocks hedge funds love to short

Catalyst watch:

  • Booking Holdings (BKNG) will begin trading on a split-adjusted basis following its 25-for-1 stock split.

  • Shareholders with Black Hills (BKH) and NorthWestern Energy (NWE) will vote on the planned merger between the companies.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Bitcoin is down 2.4% at $66,000. Gold is down 3.5% at $4,617.

The FTSE 100 is little changed and the DAX is down 1.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Stitch Fix (SFIX) +3% – Shares rose after the company resumed share repurchases under its existing $150M program.

Economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Atlantic Union Bankshares Poised For Continued Healthy Growth
data.description:
This article serves as an update on my previous analysis of Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB) that was published on August 12 on Seeking Alpha. At that time, the company had recently reported its Q2 2025 results, which were some of the strongest for the bank holding company in four years. I rated AUB as a Buy based not only on the company’s improving profitability but also some recent insider optimism that had become evident during some buying in July and August.

Since that analysis was posted on this site, Atlantic Union stock is up 10.1% when dividends are included. That gain is just over 8% better than the S&P 500 and about 2% ahead of the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE). My Buy recommendation was looking even better in early February when AUB was trading for over $41 per share. As I write this update today, its share price is just a few cents above $36.

After reading through the results from Q3 2025 and Q4 2025, there is little to dim my optimism for Atlantic Union in the year ahead. I think the banking operations of the company are growing at a healthy pace and operate in an area that is an active territory for regional banking mergers and acquisitions. Regional banks that are taken over in AUB's service area are being bought at a premium to the overall market, and for all of these reasons, I am maintaining my Buy rating on Atlantic Union Bankshares.

Company Overview

Atlantic Union Bankshares operates under the name Atlantic Union Bank, with 178 branch locations in Maryland, Virginia, and North Carolina. The holding company also operates additional financial services companies including Atlantic Union Equipment Finance, Atlantic Union Financial Consultants, and Union Insurance Group. In its Q4 2025 earnings presentation, AUB referred to itself as the largest regional bank headquartered in the Lower Mid-Atlantic region of the US. The complete geographic footprint of the bank can be seen below along with its current assets, loans, and deposits.

Atlantic Union ended last year with $37.6 billion in total assets. Included in that total were gross loans valued at $27.8 billion, investment securities worth $5.1 billion, and a cash position of $966 million. Year-over-year growth in these numbers are significant but must also factor in the addition of Sandy Spring Bancorp, a regional bank with operations in Virginia and Maryland that was acquired in April of 2025 for approximately $1.3 billion.

The loan portfolio owned by Atlantic Union is heavily weighted toward commercial loans, specifically commercial real estate. Investor CRE comprised 25.8% of the total loan mix at the end of 2025. Owner-occupied CRE stood at 15.5%, bringing the sum of those two segments to just over 41%. The bank also held a sizeable amount of home mortgages and HELOCS, as shown in the table below.

Total deposits held by the bank equaled $30.5 billion at the end of last year. About 22% of those deposits were in non-interest-bearing accounts. With additional large allocations to low-interest checking and money market accounts, Atlantic Union was able to maintain a cost of deposits of 2.03% in Q4 2025 which was nine basis points below the national average for that quarter according to the most recent data available from the FDIC.

As I mentioned in my previous analysis of the company, dividends are an important part of the justification for supporting an investment in AUB. Last year was the 15th consecutive year that the company has raised its total annual distribution. The company currently pays out $0.37 to its shareholders on a quarterly basis. That is a $0.03 increase from the $0.34 that was being paid back in August. The stock has a forward yield of 4.14%, over a full percentage point more than its sector average.

Analysis of Atlantic Union Bankshares

Atlantic Union reported its Q4 2025 and FY 2025 financial report on January 22. Adjusted earnings of $0.97 per share were $0.11 better than expected and $0.30 higher than in Q4 2024. For the full year, adjusted operating earnings of $444.8 million resulted in adjusted EPS of $3.44. GAAP earnings for FY 2025 were admittedly lower due to costs associated with the acquisition of Sandy Springs Bancorp.

When I last wrote about AUB, expectations for FY 2025 adjusted earnings were for $3.16 per share, so the $3.44 that was reported showed the pace of improvements that the company was able to make in both Q3 and Q4. Atlantic Union is projected to make $3.76 per share in FY 2026 and $4.05 in FY 2027. Both of those results would put the stock in single-digit PE territory at its current market price.

For the last three months of 2025, the bank was able to show improvement in every key performance metric shown below. When non-recurring costs were removed from the equation, Atlantic Union recorded return on assets of 1.50% and return on equity of 11.33%. These numbers reflected gains of 20 and 133 basis points, respectively, from Q3 in those two important ratios. The bank’s efficiency ratio dropped from an already impressive 48.79% in Q3 to 47.77% in Q4. Net interest margin jumped 23 basis points in Q4 from the previous three months to land just short of 4%.

Q4 2025

Q3 2025

Q4 2024

ROA (Adjusted)

1.50%

1.30%

1.03%

ROE (Adjusted)

ROTCE (Adjusted)

Efficiency Ratio (Adjusted)

3.90%

3.26%

Cost of Deposits

2.03%

2.18%

2.48%

Asset quality for AUB looks good as well. The company’s ratio of non-performing assets to total loans was just 0.42% at the end of 2025. That is a seven-basis point improvement from the end of the previous quarter. Net charge-offs in the final quarter were almost zero at 0.01%. Past due loans that were still accruing were at 0.41%.

The table below was included as part of AUB’s Q4 2025 earnings presentation to give investors a look at what 2026 could bring. Loan growth of roughly 6% and deposit growth of 5% seem reasonable and should support healthy expansion of the company’s bottom line assuming NIM and other operational metrics are achieved as expected. Growth in the company's tangible book value of 12 to 15% also tracks with the company’s recent results.

It should be noted that Atlantic Union did create these estimates with the assumption that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates twice this year, including once in April. That is looking increasingly unlikely as higher gas prices are making inflation even more challenging to control. This could make NIM expansion more challenging than expected. However, that is a concern that every bank in the US will grapple with throughout 2026 and not a problem that is going to impact Atlantic Union in a vacuum. The company's next earnings report should have minimal lingering effects from the Sandy Spring merger, giving investors a clearer look at how the bank will be able to perform in the current year.

Additional Risks to Consider

As with most regional banks, a lack of geographical diversity is a potential added risk for investing in Atlantic Union Bankshares. While I tend to believe that opportunities in Virginia and North Carolina are potential strengths for the company, the area in Northern Virginia and Maryland, especially around Washington, DC, could be more challenging. This part of the country is highly dependent on the federal government for growth in jobs, wages, and business activity. Multiple government shutdowns on top of cuts in many federal programs have created headwinds for economic development in an area where 11% of the total employment is tied to the federal government, according to the US Bureau of Labor and Statistics. Softer tourism activity is layering on additional potential challenges for the DC area where total payrolls in both the private and public sectors have declined, year over year.

As mentioned earlier with the reference to interest rate cuts and the Fed, there are a number of other expectations in the regional banking sector that have been altered in just the last couple of months. Inflation appears to be staying higher for longer. Long-term rates are moving up when they were expected to move down. The odds of a recession later this year are approaching 40%. The macro environment appears to be making it more challenging for banks to grow profits meaningfully in 2026, and smaller banks like AUB may come under even greater pressure as they have less cushion to withstand issues with repayment of commercial loans and declining consumer deposits. I addressed many of these issues earlier this week when I wrote an analysis of the State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF, and I would encourage investors who may be new to regional banks to read through that text for additional details about the risks involved with any investment in this industry.

The Bottom Line

There is nothing remarkably flashy about AUB or its recent results, but there is also no glaring weakness. While deal-making activity may have taken a pause in the last few weeks, I expect it to pick up again soon. I have no way of knowing if Atlantic Union Bankshares will be involved in any transactions later this year, either as a buyer or as a takeover candidate itself, but an increase in activity would help support the valuations of other companies in their area, especially in an attractive market like the states where Atlantic Union Bank operates. I still believe AUB is worth a buy heading into its upcoming earnings announcement later this month.

data.title: M-Tron Industries: A Hidden RF Player Behind Missile Systems, Fairly Valued
data.description:
I am always looking to add new names to my aerospace and defense coverage. I already cover over 130 aerospace and defense stocks, but I believe there are still many more names that are worthy of further investigation for investment. A reader tipped M-Tron Industries (MPTI) as a name following the company’s price movement post-earnings. In this report, I discuss the company’s activities, the most recent results and I explain why I believe the stock is a hold despite its growth prospects but still is very close to a buy rating.

M-Tron Industries Is Niche Radio Frequency Component Manufacturer

M-Tron is a small but important provider of high-reliability RF (Radio Frequency) component manufacturer focused on frequency and spectrum solutions. You can see this as the timing and signal integrity backbone of advanced electronic systems. The company designs filters, oscillators, crystal resonators, RF/microwave assemblies and high-power amplifiers for the aerospace and defense, space & satellite communication and communication & data infrastructure markets.

The company Is not a high-volume player but provides solutions when in high-spec and high-reliability applications such as electronic warfare, radar, space systems and guided munitions. That also means that the company is positioned extremely well for missile, radar and drone proliferation with deep vertical integration as the company stretches from material science to design to manufacturing, testing and integration. M-Tron is active in the critical RF components layer placing it as a Tier 2/2.5 supplier.

As demand is increasing, M-Tron faces a ramp up challenge that it has to execute but once executed successfully there should be substantial margin upside. M-Tron faces some risk in the sense that it has customer and integration overlap with other companies. Whereas M-Tron is active in the component layer offering high customization options, there are players such as Qorvo and Mercury Systems which are active in the semiconductor and subsystem layer. From technological perspective Qorvo is a potential competitor and from system architecture level Mercury Systems is a potential competitor. This all revolves around vertical integration that could mean that one of the companies venturing into respective spaces.

So, M-Tron has to remain competitive, meaning its needs to remain on the technological frontier and also explore vertical integration opportunities and capacity expansions. In that light, the company recently announced a rights offering which could result in gross proceeds of $42 million.

Double Digit Growth For M-Tron But With Margin Pressure

In the fourth quarter, sales grew 11.2% to $14.2 million driven by strong volumes on defense programs as well as avionics. Gross margin were down 80 basis points showing a moderate headwind from tariffs. Net income grew 60.1% to $3.4 million while adjusted EBITDA grew 46.8% to $4.5 million marking a strong margin improvement from 23.9% to 31.5%. However, we note that the company had previously reserved around $0.86 million in bonuses contingent to performance milestones being met. Those milestones were not met, and management reversed the bonus providing a tailwind to earnings in Q4. Adjusted for this, the EBITDA margins would be 25.5% showing a more moderated but still meaningful step up in EBITDA margins.

For the full year, revenues increased 11% to $54.4 million with gross margins declining from 46.2% to 44.4% driven by tariffs and product mix. Net income rose 10.6% to $8.45 million while adjusted EBITDA grew 12.9% to $12.582. Adjusted EBITDA margins improved from 22.7% to 23.1%. However, last year’s figures included 3% of sales in bonuses. Adjusting for that, the margins in 2024 would have been 25.7%. So, we do see some downward pressure on the margins driven by production ramp ups and tariff headwinds.

M-Tron Industries Stock Is Fairly Valued

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

M-Tron definitely has growth prospects. I believe that sales will grow 10% annually through 2028 with 14.8% growth in EBITDA and 16.4% growth in free cash flow. What I also do like about M-Tron Industries is the fact that it has no bank debt and its capital raise through a right of purchase announced in March leaves the company with a cash position that it can use to grow inorganically. Since the company has no debt, it means there is no balance sheet risk and the only risk faced is execution risk on program ramp ups. However, from valuation perspective we see that while the company is undervalued against the aerospace and defense peers it is fairly valued against its own median EV/EBITDA. So, for now I am assigning a hold rating. So, it is more a speculative opportunity rather than a name with a buy conviction. We do note that for some investors his may also look like an appealing buy and that would not necessarily be wrong. The scoring system is automated, so we score a stock on several metrics and the score that came out for M-Tron Industries was less than 5% off from a buy rating. So, it is not an unattractive stock. It just doesn’t meet the buy threshold by a narrow margin.

I expect that margins and sales will continue to rise with free cash flow continuing to improve strongly. Cash conversion is expected to remain strong on acceptable CapEx levels. Since the company has no debt leverage is not a concern and with proceeds from warrant exercises and capital raises the net cash position is comfortable. What we do note is that M-Tron has in some way or form make use of the cash it raises else it is just cash sitting on the balance sheet for which EPS have been diluted.

Emphasizing The Importance Of Signal And Time Integrity

While I do not believe that the stock is a buy at this point in time, I do believe it is important to note that the company does play an important role in the sense that it is positioned as a company with a product portfolio that is in demand for applications where failure is not an option. Quite often the companies that are gaining investor attention are the ones that build the fancy fighter jets and missiles, but behind that communication, time and signal integrity plays an important role and that is where M-Tron plays an important role.

In the days that I still found myself in lecture rooms for my Aerospace Engineering degree, I followed a module called Applied Numerical Analysis and Computational Modeling. As an example of the importance of these rather boring parts of the curriculum my lecturer provided an example of how residual time errors due to rounding accumulated over time inside the timer of a missile and actually would lead to missiles missing their targets.

Years later I found an example of a missile failure due to integer time values being converted to floating point leading to a cumulating rounding error over time. This was the Patriot missile failure in 1991 in Dhahran when a Patriot missile failed to intercept a Scud missile leading to 28 casualties. The internal timer had cumulated a time residual error of 0.34 seconds which multiplied by the speed of the a Scud missile meant that the tracking of the Scud missile by the Patriot missile was off by around 600 meters. Whether my lecturer had this real-world example in mind is something I do not know, but it does the importance of signal and time integrity.

Conclusion: Strong Prospects But Valuation Is Fair

I do believe that M-Tron has appealing growth prospects with growth in electronic warfare applications as well as missile defense and drones and growth in avionics markets. However, from valuation perspective I do believe that the stock is more or less fairly valued as there is no strong reason to believe that the company will trade in line with aerospace and defense peers any time soon. As a result, I am initiating coverage with a hold rating.

data.title: General Motors (GM) Stock Analysis: Why The Quant System Says "Strong Buy" | 2-Minute Analysis

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Welcome to 2-Minute Analysis. Our goal is to not only entertain but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. So let's throw two minutes on the clock and dive in.

Today we're looking at General Motors, ticker symbol GM. Kicking things off here with the Quant Rating system, it is currently a STRONG BUY rating on the stock. And we're going to dive into its metrics here in just a moment. Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha analysts, they are currently a BUY in aggregate on the stock, and that's from eight analysts providing their coverage in the last 90 days.

And lastly, Wall Street currently has a BUY rating on this stock as well, and that's 27 analysts across the street providing their coverage in the last 90 days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

Now let's dive deeper. This is a $65.97 billion market capitalization company found within the consumer discretionary sector and the automobile manufacturers industry.

Jumping into the valuation grade, it is currently a B-. We're going to kick things off here with the price-to-cash flow, which you currently see is 2.46 compared to the sector at 9.35. And that forward price-to-book is 0.99 compared to the sector at 2.15. Metrics looking pretty steady and healthy there.

Let's jump over to the growth grade, it is currently an A-. You'll notice here the EPS GAAP growth forward is currently 27.25% compared to the sector at just 10.19%. Also that operating cash flow growth year over year is 33.47% compared to the sector, which is only at 2.16%.

Jumping into the profitability grade, currently an A+. A couple things to highlight here. First off, cash from operations is $26.87 billion compared to the sector at just $327.1 million. Cash per share for this company is $23.17 compared to the sector at just $2.76. And lastly, net income margin is 1.46% compared to the sector at 3.98%.

Jumping into the momentum grade, it is an A grade. And that's because of the one-year price performance, this stock is up 54.62% while the sector is down 4.98%. Although more recently, the three-month price performance is more in line with the sector.

Jumping into the revisions, that's an A- grade. That is 19 up revisions and four down revisions for earnings per share over the last three months, and eight up revisions and nine down revisions for revenue numbers over the last three months.

Now General Motors does pay a small dividend yield of 0.99%. And that is, if we go back into the history context here, a company that cut its dividend during the COVID era and has recently reinstated the company's dividend over the last three years. However, looking into the yield as well, it is trailing the yield compared to its sector peers. So something that investors should keep in mind when they’re looking at General Motors stock.

Now come on over to Seeking Alpha, go ahead and follow General Motors, put it into your Seeking Alpha portfolio, get all the free breaking news alerts about this company. And don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2-Minute Analysis gets published.
